title: Characteristics of Coarsening Cellular Structures in 2D
speaker: Jeremy Mason

proof_stages.label: Trivalent curvature-driven cellular-network models
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up polycrystalline grains and two-dimensional foams as trivalent cellular networks with isotropic boundary energy, curvature-driven coarsening, 120-degree junctions, and the area-change law proportional to side count minus six.

proof_stages.label: Topological transition taxonomy
proof_stages.summary: The dynamics are organized by local graph-changing events: disappearance of two- and three-sided grains in grain growth, triangle/four-/five-sided bubble disappearances in foams, and edge flips, with foams exhibiting discontinuous nonlocal re-equilibration after such events.

proof_stages.label: Conjectural universal steady state
proof_stages.summary: The speaker formulates the material-science conjecture that for almost any initial condition, rescaled grain growth approaches a statistically steady, universal cellular structure, and discusses a possible local graph-isomorphism notion for making sameness precise.

proof_stages.label: Gauss-Bonnet cluster relation and Aboav-Weaire correction
proof_stages.summary: Applying Gauss-Bonnet to one grain and then summing over a cluster cancels internal curvature terms and yields a nearest-neighbor side-count relation whose zero-curvature approximation recovers the Aboav-Weaire law, leaving an open curvature-correction term.

proof_stages.label: Rational topological separation of neighboring grains
proof_stages.summary: To refine crude nearest-neighbor shells, the talk maps a rational-number tree onto the trivalent network so that relative positions of grains receive rational labels, revealing different correlations for topologically distinct neighbor classes.

proof_stages.label: Flag-stabilizer encoding of full topology
proof_stages.summary: By placing complete flags associated to cell-edge-vertex triples and using three involutive moves between flags, the speaker identifies stabilizers of base flags whose full elements reconstruct the entire topology of the cellular network.

proof_stages.label: Entropy from finite-depth flag types
proof_stages.summary: Truncating stabilizer information by maximum path length partitions base flags into local structural types, producing a normalized Shannon entropy curve that orders ordered, random, and steady-state cellular networks and appears to saturate at short path lengths.

proof_stages.label: Canonical short stabilizer paths and disorder scale
proof_stages.summary: After quotienting away trivial edge- and vertex-loop deformations, the speaker restricts to canonical disk-bounding paths and finds that the most frequent nontrivial patterns are very short, suggesting a graph-theoretic length scale for disorder.

examples.name: Side-count consequences of the von Neumann-Mullins law
examples.note: The speaker spells out that six-sided grains stay area-stationary, five- and four-sided grains shrink, and seven- or eight-sided grains grow as routine instances of the formula dA/dt proportional to n-6.
examples.engagement_reason: he applies the von Neumann-Mullins formula to specific side counts to infer six-sided grains stay fixed, five- and four-sided grains shrink at different rates, and seven/eight-sided grains grow
